parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits severe I/O inefficiency due to extremely small access granularity. With an average write size of only 64 bytes and 100% small I/O operations (small_io_ratio=1.0), the application is making excessive system calls. Writing 26.2MB of data with 64-byte operations requires ~409,600 individual write() calls across 4 processes, creating massive syscall overhead. The metadata_time_ratio of 19.16% confirms significant time spent in system call overhead rather than actual data transfer. Current bandwidth of 29.1 MB/s is severely limited by this access pattern.

recommendations.explanation: The application performs 64-byte writes in a loop, causing excessive system call overhead. Each write() call has fixed overhead regardless of size, so 409,600 tiny writes waste enormous CPU cycles in kernel transitions.

recommendations.expected_speedup: 16x-25x based on KB evidence showing dramatic improvement when moving from 64B to 1MB+ transfers

recommendations.explanation: The job's average write size of 1,965 bytes is far below the optimal 1MB+ threshold. Small I/O operations create excessive syscall overhead and prevent efficient storage utilization.
recommendations.code_before: for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
recommendations.expected_speedup: Significant improvement based on eliminating small I/O overhead

recommendations.explanation: With 32 processes writing to shared files, collective MPI-IO would be more efficient than independent POSIX operations. The benchmark shows HDF5 collective operations (which use MPI-IO underneath) as the optimal approach.
recommendations.code_before: H5Dwrite(dset, type, memspace, filespace, H5P_DEFAULT, data)  /* h5bench_write.c:~550 */
recommendations.code_after: H5Dwrite(dset, type, memspace, filespace, dxpl_coll, data)  /* h5bench_write.c:~520 */
recommendations.expected_speedup: Additional improvement from collective I/O coordination
recommendations.api_change: HDF5 independent -> HDF5 collective (or POSIX -> MPI_File_write_all)

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: Combined optimizations should provide substantial speedup by eliminating small I/O overhead and enabling collective operations, though exact speedup depends on application-specific I/O patterns

The job writes 419MB across 4 processes with small 4KB transfer sizes, achieving only 13.6 MB/s bandwidth - indicating significant inefficiency from the random access pattern.

recommendations.explanation: Random file access with 49.9% non-sequential writes is defeating OS read-ahead and storage prefetching mechanisms. Small 4KB writes in random order create excessive seek overhead and prevent efficient storage utilization.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-4x based on benchmark evidence showing dramatic improvement when converting random to sequential access

recommendations.explanation: For cases where random access cannot be avoided, switching to MPI-IO with data sieving can help aggregate small random accesses into larger sequential operations.

recommendations.code_after: MPI_File_write(fh, buf, transferSize, MPI_BYTE, &status)  /* MPI-IO with data sieving */

parsed.diagnosis: This job exhibits a file-per-process I/O pattern that is creating excessive metadata overhead. With 32 processes each creating separate files, the job is overwhelming the metadata servers with file creation operations. Despite achieving reasonable bandwidth (193 MB/s) and good sequential access patterns (99.94% sequential), the file explosion pattern is limiting scalability and creating unnecessary filesystem stress. The metadata time ratio of 1.01% indicates some metadata overhead, which would worsen significantly at larger scales.

recommendations.explanation: The job is using a file-per-process pattern where each of the 32 ranks creates its own output file. This creates 32 separate files and associated metadata operations, overwhelming the filesystem's metadata servers. At larger scales, this pattern becomes catastrophic for performance.
recommendations.expected_speedup: 2-5x based on elimination of metadata bottleneck and collective I/O optimization

parsed.overall_expected_improvement: 2-5x speedup range, with higher gains at larger process counts due to reduced metadata server contention
